So your first point covers passing file(s) by *filename*, and would go a long way to making things easier for us. We'd need to add the caveat: *when redirecting test files via `gabbi-run < test.yaml`, `<@filename` will read files relative to the current directory.*

On your second point, can you help me understand the difficulty? I envisage something like:

```python
# filename can be relative or absolute
path = os.path.join(self.test_directory, filename)
# relpath takes care of normalising path for us
has_dir_traversal = os.path.relpath(path, start=self.test_directory).startswith(os.pardir)
```

This allows the user to enter any path they like, relative or absolute, but if the relative path starts with `..` then you can't play ball. What do you think?

This has some interesting issues. It is easy enough to get the port set to 443 at the runner level, however, when we get around to actually doing the request, since `test_data['ssl'] = False` (the default), the URL is created with `http`.

Because httplib2 has this code:

```python
            (scheme, authority, request_uri, defrag_uri) = urlnorm(uri)
            domain_port = authority.split(":")[0:2]
            if len(domain_port) == 2 and domain_port[1] == '443' and scheme == 'http':
                scheme = 'https'
                authority = domain_port[0]
```

everything works. But this is an implementation detail that we should not rely on.

So I'm thinking the underlying error/issue is the way in which SSL is being managed (some of which is discussed in #50). Changing that would be a fairly fundamental thing, unless we do something like manage the test base somehow.

Ideas?

I've struggled with this in the past, failing to come up with a pattern that's both generic and reliable - so ... ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

FWIW, when tackling this, we might also want to consider media types like `application/vnd.api+json; ext=jsonpatch`.

You can also email me grassfedcode@gmail.com if you want more help. 

If you get it working feel free to close this ticket.

Actor's don't stop by themselves or if they're out of work, so if you never call `.stop()` on an actor, they will accumulate and you'll eventually hit som OS limits to how many threads you can create.

First, I apologize for the long post. I did not realize it would be this long when I started it...

I think it would be useful to separate the Measurement class from error propagation. A measurement is quantity with an uncertainty, and there are many useful features that the Measurement class can provide. Here are just a few that come to mind:

the upper bound (nominal value + uncertainty)
the lower bound (nominal value - uncertainty)
the measurement interval (upper - lower)

These are fairly simple calculations, but it would be nice if the measurement class did them. There is currently an issue with non-multiplicative units that could be resolved if the Measurement class did this, I can't do this for example:
```
T = units.Measurement( 20, 1, 'degC' )

Tmax = T.value + T.error
```
because I can't add two temperatures. These features would be more useful if you wanted to add support for non-symmetric errors.

If we assume that the measurement has Gaussian statistics (which is the only assumption that makes sense when the uncertainty is specified as a single quantity), then these features would be useful:

the 95% confidence interval for the measurement
the X% confidence interval for the measurement
are two measurements statistically different?
automatically calculate a Measurement value and error from a list of Quantities

All of these things would be useful, and are separate from error propagation. And now for error propagation...

I would argue that error propagation is like function interpolation. Most people that need to do it have some very specific requirements and end up using their own code for anything beyond very basic calculations because no available library does everything exactly the way that they need. For example, I also use my own error propagation module (https://github.com/CD3/pyErrorProp) because I teach an undergraduate level physics lab and I needed to be able to reproduce the uncertainties that our students would calculate by hand, and we do not use the derivative method. There are many cases where the derivative method simply does not work. For example, what if I want to propagate error through a function that I don't have an analytic expression for?

As @alexfeld points out, the "correct" way to propagate error is to use a Monte-Carlo algorithm. But even then there are numerous options. The "real correct" way to propagate error would be to specify a complete probability distribution for all inputs to a calculation and compute the complete probability distribution for the result. But this is usually not possible because the input probability distributions are not available (and it may take way to long to calculate). So, we have to make some assumptions. If we assume that all of the inputs are Gaussian, then we can use a Monte Carlo method to generate the result's probability distribution. But here again, we will have to make an assumption because the result's probability distribution may not be Gaussian or symmetric. So it's not possible to characterize it with just a nominal value and error.

So, my point is this. Any error propagation method you choose will likely only satisfy a minority of users. `pint` is a fantastic units library that does true dimensional analysis and is very good at going to and from strings. Having a Measurement class that extends this to uncertain quantities would be very useful. For error propagation, I would like to see an error propagation framework that is easy to extend or replace and supports propagating error through user defined functions.

Anyhow, that is my two cents. Again, sorry for the long post.

Very interesting...

1. if your `use` statement makes use of **only** module variable (parameter?) the `.o` (`.a` or `.so`) is not necessary;
2. if your `use` statement access to **also** module procedure the compiled objects other than the `.mod` are necessary!

Anyhow, I can conclude that in the general case both `.mod` and one of `.o`, `.a` or `.so` files are required. *Hic sunt leones...* while the `.mod` file are created with the name of the module the object files are commonly created with the name of source files.

Ok, I've found the origin of the problem. Because this model has a single additive Parameter, `symfit` is not able to determine the shape of the data on the level of the `Model`. (Because `Model`'s don't have knowledge about the data.)

So in `Model.elav_jacobian` there is some logic which makes sure all the components of the Jacobian have the same shape, but in this case the jacobian is only the derivative w.r.t `shift `, which is `1`. Because there is no other component, the jacobian returned is therefore [[[1]]] (lists correspond to param, component, datapoints).

But that is not correct, it should be [[ 8 * [1]]] in your example in order for it to be contracted with the weight matrix `W` in the line
`cov_matrix_inv = np.tensordot(W*jac, jac, (range(1, jac.ndim), range(1, jac.ndim)))`

Now the question is, what to do about it in light of the plans for 1.0.0, which will upgrade Variables to indexed objects, which will make it easier to prevent these issues in the first place because `Fit` can then work with a  deepcopy of the `Model` whose `Variable` shapes have been set on the bases of the data. This will make such problems a thing of the past.

So I'm now unsure wether to add a quick try/except for this specific type of problem, of to set this to wontfix because it will be fixed in the future in a far more elegant way.

As a workaround for your immediate problems btw, I can now suggest using 
```
k = symfit.Parameter('k', value=1, fixed=True)
model = {length: shift + 1 / (1 + symfit.exp(- k* temperature))}
```
instead. This way the shape can be correctly estimated.

+1 for bundling `six.py`.  It's lightweight, not changing fast (and updates are basically never required, anyway), and is convenient to use for Py2-Py3 compatible code bases.

Basically, any time you use `range`, `zip`, `StringIO`, `dict.items`/`dict.iteritems`, you should include it from `six`.  If it's going to be required, anyway, it's just as easy to bundle it explicitly as it is to require users to install the standalone copy IMO.  It's *certainly* needed of you use metaclasses.

Just pulling out the pieces you need or want to use at the current time means that every time you want to use something else (or find somewhere that using the `moves` from `six` is better for Py2), you need to pull in that extra bit of functionality into whatever you wrote in place of `six`.  And `six` is so well-tested and so widely-used that you wouldn't want to do something *else*, really.

Just my 2c.

Hey, sorry for the delay on reviewing this. Here are some rambling thoughts.

* xrange is not defined in python3, and the number of retries should be small, so I recommend changing this to range.

* `make_requests` is only called once, so I generally shy away from breaking such things out to their own functions. I am similarly wary of `get_status` but I do see it used twice. This is a preference thing somewhat.

* You don't want to `print()` deep down in the exception handling code. Should either consider adding logging as a separate effort, just removing it altogether, or otherwise indicating somehow that the rate limit got exceeded (like tracking it in a counter and returning that somewhere like in an exception if the max is reached, or at the object level).

* Also, consider the case that you make several requests using `get_all_pages`, but then in the course of handling a later request you hit an exception. What would be ideal to return would be the combined results of everything up to that point along with the pertinent error information, as the user/programmer may want to use what they got back up to that point.

* To dovetail on that last idea, what I would like is to return something in the exception that would allow the programmer to examine the contents and actually pick up where they left off if they wanted. For example, they make some requests with `get_all_pages`, hit the rate limit, then hit the total wait time or retry limits, but they decide after looking at the exception contents "I would still like to wait and retry to finish this" in their application code. Maybe this doesn't make much sense; maybe there should be a "I want to wait indefinitely" option and perhaps that should be the default.

Yes, to create the meta features for the meta-model, "#base_model * #CV" models are effectively trained. However, once the meta-model is fit to the meta-features, the base models are refit to the original training data, i.e., we have #base_models in the end for prediction, not "#base_model * #CV".

To summarize the procedure in pseudo code:

---

1) train the number of trained regressors is: base-regressors * cv_folds  

For each base regressor b:   
    For iteration in cross-validation:   
        fit b to training k-1 folds (training folds)  
        use b to make prediction on the kth fold (holdout fold); aka meta feats  

2) use meta feats to fit level 2 regressor ('meta regressor')  

3) fit each base regressor to the original data

---  

I hope this answers your question :)

I'm not an expert on choosing color blind friendly color palettes, but I've run across this topic in the seaborn documentation before (see https://seaborn.pydata.org/tutorial/color_palettes.html#qualitative-color-palettes). Seaborn has a built in `colorblind` palette (shown below) that could be of use here

![colorblind](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11656932/37481440-c4b0411a-284f-11e8-86bb-46bcdf55964d.png)

Here's a code snippet to reproduce [example 3 in the `plot_decision_regions` user guide](http://rasbt.github.io/mlxtend/user_guide/plotting/plot_decision_regions/#example-3-decision-region-grids) using the seaborn `colorblind` palette:

```python
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB 
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn import datasets
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from mlxtend.plotting import plot_decision_regions
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
import itertools

# Load and format colorblind palette for use in plot_decision_regions
color_pal = sns.color_palette("colorblind", 6).as_hex()
colors = ','.join(color_pal)

# Initializing Classifiers
clf1 = LogisticRegression(random_state=1)
clf2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)
clf3 = GaussianNB()
clf4 = SVC()

# Loading some example data
iris = datasets.load_iris()
X = iris.data[:, [0,2]]
y = iris.target

gs = gridspec.GridSpec(2, 2)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))

labels = ['Logistic Regression', 'Random Forest', 'Naive Bayes', 'SVM']
for clf, lab, grd in zip([clf1, clf2, clf3, clf4],
                         labels,
                         itertools.product([0, 1], repeat=2)):

    clf.fit(X, y)
    ax = plt.subplot(gs[grd[0], grd[1]])
    fig = plot_decision_regions(X=X, y=y, clf=clf, colors=colors, legend=2)
    plt.title(lab)

plt.show()
```

Thanks for thinking about this so thoroughly, and I agree with your concerns :). Like you suggested, the best way might be to store the training meta features as an attribute. Although, it may seem a bit awkward, it's also done in the scikit-learn API.

However, instead of passing an argument to `fit`, I would suggest to do it in the constructor, which can potentially avoid other complications down the road (e.g., if someone wants to use the StackingCVRegressor as part of a scikit-learn pipeline, which calls fit without extra arguments).

So, what I am suggesting is something like

```python
StackingCVRegressor(..., store_train_meta_features=False)`
```

and the attribute would be `train_meta_features_`, similar to what's been done in scikit-learn in e.g., the `LinearDiscriminantAnalysis` with the `store_covariance` argument and `covariance_`

In addition, I would still suggest to have a method

```python
predict_meta_features(self, X)
```
to compute meta features for e.g., the test set and other datasets.

the concern is that we don't know the capacity and possible throughput of pyspider since we don't know what step takes most resources in order to optimize it.

For example - we have approximately 70 projects
we are able to process approximately 1,300,000 pages per day on a server:
32 cores, 128gb ram, ssd disks, couple of lan cards and 300 proxy servers

what we don't know is that:
1. is one of the projects not implemented well or can be optimized and it utilizes much more resources than all other projects?
2. what part of the project (on a per project basis) is the slowest (ie may be able to optimize if knew where the problem is)
3. if we add more servers - what part do we need to offload - processors, result_workers, etc. because we don't know which one needs more help than others
4. time it takes to retrieve page - possibly just the site is slow and we can't pull more info, or check if maybe we are now getting throttled if up until today we had 100 times the speed on retrieving the page
5. time it takes to store data from result_worker - ie - connection to DB server is overloaded
6. time it takes to pull job from queue - maybe redis is misconfigured or overutilized and takes too long to serve out the queue
7. time it takes to update tasks and results collections to see if maybe some collections have grown to large or indexes got corrupted and we don't know about the changes.
8. how many times each process was fired today and to be able to compare it to previous days, weeks, etc. to monitor fluctuations - possibly got blocked and receive a lot more HTTP errors than ever before
9. how many errors (with error content / stacktrace) happened and logged them all
10. and much much more actionable info can be acquired and utilized to turn pyspider into a next-gen platform.

Really resource utilization logging is the only missing part from pyspider as it stands today. It's a great platform, very fast and very extensible, but without transparency into what happens behind the scenes - it is not likely to get picked up by many large organizations as the platform of their choice.

As far as what it takes to implement it - it can be easily done with the backwards compatibility:
add config option for log_driver and log_options. Have only one push driver originally, we can contribute more plugins for it as soon as you have it.
if they logging params are specified, than push data to end point in POST or JSON format. 
End point listener should already be a separate micro/service - ie elasticsearch, file, fluentd, sysdig, etc.. All these fun tools have interfaces like kibana, graylog2/grafana, and so many more.

As an improved alternative - all these "messages" could be pushed to REDIS or get aggregated in memory to be offloaded in chunks when chunk reaches nn entries or every yy seconds

So I ran your data and looked at what happens.  The issue where the
training loss goes to 0 but the detector doesn't find the object is due to
border effects. Which are somewhat extreme with the default settings I
have. Basically what happens is that objects that are too close to the edge
of the image are impossible to detect.  This is because each possible
object location is centered on a pixel in the output feature map (see
http://blog.dlib.net/2017/08/vehicle-detection-with-dlib-195_27.html for a
discussion).  But what happens is the edge of the image gets whittled away
a little bit each time you go down the image pyramid, so that really large
objects can't be detected if they are near the edge of the image.

If you simply pad the image with 0s the detector will find your objects. I
could make this padding automatic but it makes the detector run slower.  So
I leave this up to users to do if they need it.  I guess this could be more
clear.

Anyway, none of what I just said is relevant for training. The issue there
is that the network isn't powerful enough (or your data isn't well
labeled).  Add more layers to the output network.

It's not the case that the default keyring is insecure. The actual implementation prefers the most secure key ring available based on the environment.

On Mac OS and Windows systems, the system security provider is used, and they provide high-security.

On Linux, the situation is more complicated. There are secure providers for gnome and KDE, but those are only available in the GUI.

The fallback of course is the encrypted key ring, but that requires PyCrypto to be installed.

I agree there's a use case here to be considered, and that use case is, as you describe, for a new, novice user. Maybe you're right, and The only time the plaintext key ring should be used is when it's explicitly selected in configuration.

Such a change would affect users on the next systems where access to the file system is not a concern, such as with encrypted file systems, isolated backend systems, or non-shared systems.

Installing netmiko on Windows
Netmiko is a Python module by Kirk Byers that provides a wrapper around the Paramiko SSH module for doing screen scraping and CLI automation on network devices.

Paramiko has some dependencies that make installation on Windows a tad tricky. Here's a quick way to get it done:

Install Anaconda.
From the Anaconda shell, run "conda install paramiko".
From the Anaconda shell, run "pip install scp".
Install git for Windows.
Clone netmiko with "git clone https://github.com/ktbyers/netmiko"
cd into the netmiko directory and run "python setup.py install".

From the top of my head, this is similar to what I tested for small IOS and ASA devices. Note, I didn't have any auto detection of the nature of the remote device.

1. Determine size and MD5 of file on local file system.
2. SSH into remote device and verify sufficient space available on the file system. 
3. Check if device already has the new image on the file system. 
4. Check if the device is already running the new image.
5a. Archive current device configuration
5b. Transfer new image to device using SCP
6. Probably will want to make some SCP optimizations on some Cisco devices as file transfers by default are very slow.
7. Verify MD5 of image now on the device.
8. Set boot image in config. 
9. Verify boot variable, if appropriate.
10. Reload.
-----
11. Operational verification of device.
12. Flag any configuration changes
13. Verify device is actually running the new image.

This tool NEEDS to write files (for config, log, cache, ...) grouped inside a folder named `isbntools` or `.isbntools`. That is why is advisable to install it inside a virtual environment and not install it system wide. If you don't need or don't want it to write files, probably you don't need `isbntools`just use `isbnlib`. 

Some systems (see #87) don't allow the installer to write files in the user's space and some installers (`conda` ?) seem not to do it too. In that case the only solution is to create the folder manually (see #87 again).

Why don't you make all these files optional? In some way they are, however they need to be defined first by the installer and the user, location, permissions and protections be established. Then you can choose what to use. The alternative is to run the code for this *each time you run any* `isbntools` *command*!

So here's my findings. Linux (not sure about other UNIXes) by default reserves 5% of total disk space for the root user so that if, say, the user home directory fills the disk, the root user and the system in general will still have 5% of space to write into /var/log and not crash in general:
http://unix.stackexchange.com/a/7964/168884

 `tune2fs` utility can be used to find the exact value of the reserved block, which is what psutil would need in order to calculate the correct percent value:
```
~/svn/psutil {master}$ sudo tune2fs  -l /dev/sda6 | grep "Reserved block" 
Reserved block count:     4607763
```

Parsing tune2fs output is a no-no thought: I just don't want to rely on it. I tried to take a look at tune2fs source code which is available here: http://git.kernel.org/cgit/fs/ext2/e2fsprogs.git. It determines this information via a `ext2fs_r_blocks_count()` function defined in `./lib/ext2fs/blknum.c` but it's not clear to me how the struct it uses is filled. Relying on tune2fs lib is also a no-no as it would require installing the C headers separately (`sudo apt-get install e2fslibs-dev` on ubuntu).

As for `df` utility. `df` is also able to print the right percentage information so it must take the reserved blocks value from somewhere. I tried to strace it (`strace df`) but I didn't notice anything interesting. The strace output is pretty short, suggesting `df` doesn't do anything particularly complex but unfortunately it seems the information doesn't come from the `/proc` fs. I will try to take a look at `df` source code later.

Another open question is what to do with other UNIX platforms. Is the reserved block something which exists in Linux only? If not how can we determine its value on other UNIX platforms?
It looks like the first output was run on a Pentium 1 as both `process_iter()` (which internally calls `create_time()` and `name()` are unbelievably slow. Is this an old PC?
As for the open files, the OS providing this information have to keep a cache of fd/inode/path around, and update it if the file is moved/hardlinked/etc - OpenBSD just doesnt do this.

Assuming that:
* the peak rss usage for all processes can be determined on Linux only (via /proc/pid/status)
* all UNIX can already use `resource.getrusage`

So... the `@run_in_reactor` and `wait()` API are designed such that you can call `wait()` multiple times. That means that when `wait()` raises a `TimeoutError` it cannot know whether it should cancel the underlying `Deferred` or not, since it might be called again.

If the interaction pattern is "if timeout is hit we're done", perhaps you want to use `@wait_for(timeout=x)`? That will do the cancellation when timeout is hit. Or to put it another way, why is `@wait_for` insufficient for this use case?

I simply need an offline way to append local time to a csv file with
lat/lon/timestamp (UTC). (Actually in my case it's a bunch of csv files at
once so I use glob.) I am doing analysis in R, and so in/out csv means I
can call it from R with 'system(python tzwhere.py)' and then call
'read_csv()" again easily to get the results back in. I've done a quick and
dirty implementation like so:

```
from tzwhere import tzwhere
import glob
import pandas as pd

def reader(f):
  d = pd.read_csv(f, names=['id', 'latitude', 'longitude', 'timestamp'])
  return d


def main():
  files = glob.glob('*.csv')
  data = pd.concat([reader(f) for f in files], ignore_index=True)
  tz = tzwhere.tzwhere(shapely=True)
  data['local_tz'] = data.apply(lambda row: tz.tzNameAt(row['latitude'],
                                                row['longitude']), axis=1)

  data.to_csv('to_r.csv', index=False)

if __name__ == "__main__":
  main()
```

Oh I see this now.

You have an event handler that blocks for a long time.  When ``on_message`` with a message containing "!start" is received, it blocks the execution in the two ``time.sleep(30)`` statements.  When an event handler blocks the execution, no other events will be processed, and thus, no new messages will be handled either.  Without testing your code I can give some insights to your questions.

1. Because it raises an exception.  Your debug output shows the role list being equal to the user list, and thus it can not be 3 elements longer than the user list.  This of course only leads to more questions than it answers, but the gist of it is that exceptions in event handlers are silently ignored.  This is one of the poorly documented pitfalls of the Discord.py api.  If you want to see them being ignored, you can add

        import logging
        logging.basicConfig()

    at the top of your script.  An alternative is to define the ``on_error`` event to be ``raise`` which would propagate the exception all the way out of your script.  Adding ``level=logging.DEBUG`` as  an argument to ``basicConfig`` will show a lot more diagnostic to the console.

2. Your ``on_message`` event handler blocks when creating the ``Game`` object.  Discord.py processes each event one by one, and new messages and events will not be process before the current event is done.  Thus you will not see any new messages during the execution of a single event.

3. I can't quite answer that without investigating further, but I do know that throwing exceptions while doing ``client.run()`` is ill defined currently, due to some weird threading setup in discord.py.

4. This is indeed a bad way to do it, as it cannot possibly work inside an event handler.  I unfortunately don't know of any good way to handle timeouts, and other events that flow on their own.  A possible solution would be to have no timeout, and instead have ``on_message`` set a flag on input somewhere, and then the next time ``on_message`` is fired, check the flag and process it as the list of users.

One of the major changes to version 1.1 was the fact that the configuration items can be modified at runtime (if a developer wishes to do so, like a user secret and so on) and because of that, accessing the config variables are now function calls instead of direct reference, so:

```python
self.config.refresh_token_name
```

Now becomes:

```python
self.config.refresh_token_name()
```

And everything related to configuration keys follows the same principle. Please, [take a look here](http://sanic-jwt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pages/changelog.html#version-1-1-2018-06-03) for more information regarding (breaking) changes on version 1.1 and feel free to request any clarification regarding this subject :smiley:

The things that you referenced first i.e.

```python
# A boolean algebra is defined by its base elements (=domain), its operations
# (in this case only NOT, AND and OR) and an additional "symbol" type.
Algebra = collections.namedtuple("Algebra",
                                 ("domain", "operations", "symbol"))

# Defines the two base elements TRUE and FALSE for the algebra.
BooleanDomain = collections.namedtuple("BooleanDomain", ("TRUE", "FALSE"))

# Defines the basic boolean operations NOT, AND and OR.
BooleanOperations = collections.namedtuple("BooleanOperations",
                                           ("NOT", "AND", "OR"))
```

allow you to redefine all the elements of the Boolean algebra. This can't be done with inheritance since for example if you want to redefine the AND class then sub-classing it won't help because the operation `a & b` defined between two symbols doesn't know that it should use the new AND type. The way to use it is at the moment is a bit lacking since one has to dynamically set the class variable Expression.algebra as it is done here

```python
# Create a default algebra.
DOMAIN = BooleanDomain(TRUE=TRUE, FALSE=FALSE)
OPERATIONS = BooleanOperations(NOT=NOT, AND=AND, OR=OR)
ALGEBRA = Algebra(DOMAIN, OPERATIONS, Symbol)
Expression.algebra = ALGEBRA
```

for the default algebra.

Changing class variables is a tricky and not recommended thing to do since it means that there is no simple way to use two boolean algebras in parallel. Especially bad is this if two separate projects rely on this module. If one of them changes something it will influence the other one in unpredictable ways. However, it's simple and straightforward and will probably be enough for most use cases. As you noticed this feature is not well tested, e.g. the parse function doesn't make use of custom algebras. There is definitely room for improvement.

I think that PyICU and especially ICU itself is kind of a pain to install and does not contain as much information as the pdt locales, so very glad to see that you are working on this! For example, taking a look at [French in ICU](http://www.localeplanet.com/icu/fr/) you can see things like decimal separators, weekday and month names, abbreviations, meridian. As by now you have probably discovered the locale metadata specific to parsedatetime like number names, units, next/last modifiers are not provided by ICU. 

What ICU does handle well are well-formatted absolute dates and some of the regional intricacies thereof. For example, [Canadian French](http://www.localeplanet.com/icu/fr-CA/) uses a different date format than [Paris French](http://www.localeplanet.com/icu/fr-FR/). However, ICU does not capture every nuance. In Canada you will see all three mm/dd/yyyy, dd/mm/yyyy, yyyy/mm/dd formats used depending on language and context.

The primary use case where ICU makes sense is if a developer needs to process text in various source locales and that processing will be done from a centralized system on which ICU could be installed. I walked one of my Japanese users through ICU setup in the past and after some aggravation it was working, but not providing much benefit in the area of natural language date processing that parsedatetime (and by extension my own project) was known for.

I decided for the time being to implement this outside of pdt. I would like to incorporate it at some point, hopefully I will find the time. In case anyone else wants to take it on, here is what I am using.

```
re_financial_date = r'\b(?P<day>\w+) +of +\b(?P<modifier>\w+)? *\b(?P<unit>\w+)'
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta

def beginning_of_quarter(date):
    month = int(math.ceil(date.month/3.0) * 3 - 2)
    return datetime.date(date.year, month, 1)

def parse_financial_date(dateString, sourceTime):
    """
    https://github.com/bear/parsedatetime/issues/206
    :param dateString:
    :param sourceTime: date or datetime object
    :return: date object
    """
    inner_modifiers = {
        'begin': 1,
        'start': 1,
        'end': lambda x: x, # will get inner_length
    }

    pdt_constants = PdtConstants()


    re_inner = r'^(\d+|{0}).*'.format('|'.join(inner_modifiers.keys()))

    dateString = dateString.lower()
    groups = re.search(re_financial_date, dateString).groupdict()

    inner_modifier_match = re.search(re_inner, groups.get('day') or '')
    if inner_modifier_match:
        try:
            inner_offset = inner_modifiers[inner_modifier_match.groups()[0]]
        except KeyError:
            inner_offset = int(inner_modifier_match.groups()[0])
    else:
        inner_offset = 0

    outer_offset = pdt_constants.Modifiers.get(groups['modifier'], 0)

    unit = groups.get('unit')

    today = local_date(sourceTime)

    if unit  in pdt_constants.units['months']:
        outer_length = 1 # months
        base = today.replace(day=1) + relativedelta(months=outer_offset * outer_length)
        inner_length = pdt_constants.daysInMonth(base.month, base.year)

    elif unit == 'quarter':
        outer_length = 3
        base = beginning_of_quarter(today) +  relativedelta(months=outer_offset * outer_length)
        inner_length = 0
        for m in range(3):
            inner_length +=  pdt_constants.daysInMonth(base.month + m, base.year)
    elif unit in pdt_constants.units['years']:
        outer_length = 12
        base = today.replace(day=1, month=1) + relativedelta(months=outer_offset * outer_length)
        inner_length = 365
        if base.year in pdt_constants._leapYears:
            inner_length += 1
    else:
        raise ValueError('Could not derive relative date from "%s"' % dateString)

    if callable(inner_offset):
        inner_offset = inner_offset(inner_length)

    return base +  datetime.timedelta(days = inner_offset - 1)


```

That's the gist of it, yes. But the details are a bit more complex: 
it doesn't always make sense to use a list as a container. Here is the rationale:

  - if you call say `stream.read(bool)`, you *probably* don't want a list of one bool,
    but one bool, so that's what we return. If you actually want a list, you can
    use `stream.read(bool,n=1)` instead. So the return type depends on `n`
    (more precisely on `n` being `None` or not).

  - for "container types" (`str`, `BitStream`), you don't want a list either.
    For example `stream.read(str, 10)` shall return a string of length 10,
    not a list of 10 strings of length 1.
   
  - when you deal with fixed-sized integers (or floating-point numbers),
    especially a large number of them, it makes more sense to use
    NumPy arrays than lists. 

  - of course if you use custom types (that are user-defined), all bets are off. 
    The documentation of the custom type reader should provide the information. 

The "Built-in types" and "Custom types" sections of the documentation discuss this
and are referred to in the "See Also" section of the method,
but I agree that it's not clear (or fast enough ...).

For quick access, I have added a table in the documentation of `read` method 
that provides the type of `data` for builtin types (133246d2586c42228e31d4e6643753bc3ad2d0ea).
I guess that it was the kind of info you were searching for.

There are a few things to address.

---

I want to be very clear that `natsort` *does not* implement it's own less than operator. Rather, it takes the input and converts it into a tuple of strings and numbers. Python natively knows how to compare these.

---

To sort naturally without using a key function, the correct approach would be to insert the `natsort` key directly into your `__lt__` implementation.

```python
import natsort

natsort_key = natsort.natsort_keygen()

class Myclass(object):
    def __init__(self, arg):
        self.arg = arg

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return str(self) == str(other)

    def __lt__(self, other):
        return natsort_key(str(self)) < natsort_key(str(other))
```
If this approach is taken for both classes, then one can use `sorted` and it will sort naturally because the comparison operator is implemented in terms of a natural comparison - the transformation of the data happens inside `__lt__` and calling `natsorted` is not needed. Note that I don't recommend *only* implementing `__lt__` for a class - take a look at  [`total_ordering`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.total_ordering).

---

> But would nice if `natsort` had a different behavior than this unexpected error when implemented a custom iterator for a class and directly used `natsorted()` without a key.

Please describe what behavior you would want instead.  Keep in mind the following when coming up with a behavior:

 - It is not the `natsort` library that is throwing the error but rather is [the `sorted` function itself](https://github.com/SethMMorton/natsort/blob/571d23c3e25304d3fe68c87d50e3a1829989fe9c/natsort/natsort.py#L299).
 - Comparing an iterable and a non-iterable is typically not possible in python (3) -- it is only because these are custom classes that this is allowed.
 - I have taken great care to make sure that `natsort` introduces as little overhead as possible, so any solutions should not introduce significant extra overhead.

---

While I empathize with having to spend an hour trying to debug the `TypeError`, I had spent over a week (nights and weekends) writing the [How it Works](http://natsort.readthedocs.io/en/master/howitworks.html) page in the docs in the hopes that users of the library would read it and have an understanding of how `natsort` works so that they could debug issues that arise or learn how to hack the algorithm.  If this page is not enough to have explained that the `TypeError` arose from the transformation of data, please help by suggesting how that page can be improved or how the README could be improved so that what was going on and how to solve the issue would be more clear.

Looks like the way SQLAlchemy stores ORM objects with the same class name in its registry breaks some of the assumptions made in sqlservice about how to reference them. Essentially, SQLAlchemy stores these in an iterable object, `sqlalchemy.ext.declarative.clsregistry._MultipleClassMarker`, which would need to be "unpacked" with something like `list(<MultipleClassMarker>)`.

So to make this type of usage (i.e. support duplicate class names defined in separate modules), I'd just need to unpack these `MultipleClassMarker` instances and add them to sqlservice's internal model map. But having duplicate class names would break how `db.<Model>` works as it would be an ambiguous reference to the underlying model class. I'll have to think more on this, but suffice to say I _will_ be to at least make it so that `db.save(my.TestObj)` and `db.save(his.TestObj)` would work.

The TradeAnalyzer uses the "auto"dict feature in which by subclassing a dict and overriding __missing__ (basis for collections.defaultdict) a key can be inserted in real time.

If no trades have been made the keys do obviously not exist.

When a backtesting run comes to an end, the "autodict" is "_closed" which prevents the creation of new keys and therefore avoids potential typing errors. For example for a run with some trades executed and if you had typed:

```python
print(trade_analysis['total']['totol'])
```
and the "autodict" were not yet "_closed" you would actually get an "AutoDict" instance automatically created.

This would be unwanted behaviour, imho, because the typing error is better detected with a "_closed" AutoDict generating an error.

A possible compromise would be to have exactly this value: ``['total']['total']`` filled with a default value of 0, to enable a check in user code.

Unless you have any other suggestion I will add it at once.

```
permission_classes = [ConditionalPermission, TokenHasReadWriteScope]
```

Yes, you are right - the passcode is always passed as a string to the commandline by using single quotation characters `'`. This way white-space characters can be ignored in the passphrase, which is neat.

There is a problem, though, if the user himself uses the single quotation character in the passcode - this way he can end the string (effectively closing the string input). Then the shell will execute whatever comes thereafter. Example:

I'm in folder `/foo` which contains the file `bar` and run:

```
crypto bar
```

as a passphrase I choose:

```
test' --no --dryrun; rm secret_file2.txt; #'
```

Note the two quotation characters (after `test` and after `#`), which allow me to add any code to the shell execution. I then add the `gpg` commands `--no` and `--dryrun`, which will shut down any queries gpg may present. Afterwards I end the command with `;` and go on to delete a file.

The shell will now execute:

```
gpg [--some_options] --passphrase 'test' --no --dryrun; 
rm secret_file2.txt; # [--other_now_irrelevant_options]
```

Sounds like one way to fix this 'attack' is to check for single quotation marks inside the passcode and escape them properly (`\'` should work) or throw an error for these cases.

Python doc, however, discourages the use of `shell=True` all together for `subprocess` calls. Not sure if this `Naked` framework has implemented this, but instead of passing a full string to the shell, we would have to create a list with the exact commands and then call `subprocess.call(['gpg','--passcode',pswd_string,...], ...)` if this makes sense.

This extracts everything after the sixth set of whitespace in the output of `df`, so is robust against whitespace in the mountpoint:
```bash
df -P "${PWD}" | tail -1 | awk '{ for(i=6; i<NF; i++) printf "%s",$i OFS; if(NF) printf "%s",$NF; printf ORS}'
```
e.g.
```bash
$ pwd
/Volumes/Time Machine Backups/Backups.backupdb
$ df -P "${PWD}" | tail -1 | awk '{ for(i=6; i<NF; i++) printf "%s",$i OFS; if(NF) printf "%s",$NF; printf ORS}'
/Volumes/Time Machine Backups
```

Verified working out of the box on macOS 10.12 and Ubuntu Linux. This approach depends on the `-P` switch of `df` ensuring that the mountpoint is in the 6th column. I don't have access to other systems to test but it seems to be cross-platform.

That said, personally I'm +1 for depending on coreutils. It's a standard dependency on Linux, and is trivially installable (and can be specified as a dependency) using homebrew on macOS, which is presumably how a lot of people install autoenv in the first place. Failing that, if you don't want a hard dependency you could use `stat` when it is available:

```bash
if command -v stat >/dev/null 2>&1; then  # Standard GNU coreutils systems
    _mountpoint=$(stat -c %m -- "${PWD}")
elif command -v gstat >/dev/null 2>&1; then  # macOS homebrew coreutils
    _mountpoint=$(gstat -c %m -- "${PWD}")
else  # fragile but POSIX
    _mountpoint=$(df -P "${PWD}" | tail -1 | awk '{ for(i=6; i<NF; i++) printf "%s",$i OFS; if(NF) printf "%s",$NF; printf ORS}')
fi
```

`start_urls` started as a simple FIFO queue. Then someone needed to discard duplicates in the start urls queue and therefore set support was added.

Yes, you need to overwrite the `start_requests` (this method https://github.com/rolando/scrapy-redis/blob/v0.6.8/src/scrapy_redis/spiders.py#L75) method to support a priority queue (sorted set). You can also support a complex object (json-like) instead of a single string to pass additional parameters (like priority) and then pass that priority to the further requests. The usual approach in scrapy is to use `request.meta["parameter"] = value` before returning the request and in the callback read it as `response.meta["parameter"]`.

@makelove Start URLs, by default, are expected to be strings. At the other hand, serialized requests can be bytes.

The type error is there to stop processing a bad queue. It can happen when the user uses the wrong queue (i.e.: serialized requests).

I see quite a few problems here:

1. This will break Python 2.7 compatibility due to the use of "yield from" and not supporting the Trollius backport (which AsyncIOScheduler *does*)
1. This will break backwards compatibility because jobs AsyncIOExecutor now always runs everything in the event loop thread where it previously ran jobs in the default (thread pool) executor
1. BasePoolExecutor submits a task pointing to a bound method, which will break process pool based executors (this is the reason `run_job()` was a free function)
1. It duplicates a lot of code, which I was specifically hoping to avoid

Stomp.py should hopefully be pretty close to the protocol.

Heartbeats are used to keep the connection open, but in this case you've got a heartbeat timeout.  You've setup 4 sec heartbeating, and after 6 seconds it's timed out (taking into account a grace period):

```
Heartbeat timeout: diff_receive=6.00170162296854, time=640929.292792792, lastrec=640923.291091169
```

According to the stomp spec:

* the sender MUST send new data over the network connection at least every <n> milliseconds
* if, inside a time window of at least <n> milliseconds, the receiver did not receive any new data, it MAY consider the connection as dead

Then you can pick colors from any sRGB palette or GUI (HSV wheel, etc) and have the colorfulness squished and transformed under that warm illuminant. How hard/disruptive would it be for git-annex to include a small Python program which used the internal Wormhole API directly? That API is pretty stable, whereas the CLI tool's behavior is more up in the air (I know localization is a problem too).

One nuisance is that if you're currently using plain `wormhole send` (even with `--text`), you're using a particular protocol (i.e. the set of messages sent over the wormhole connection, and how they're interpreted) that was designed to accomodate sending files too. So the little python program will be more complicated than strictly necessary.

That said, I think the `src/wormhole/xfer_util.py` utility function might interoperate with the regular `wormhole send --text` protocol, so maybe the git-annex helper script could just call `wormhole.xfer_util.send()` and wait for the Deferred to fire. The script around that should only be a few lines, something like (caution, not tested):

```
from twisted.internet.task import react
from wormhole.cli.public_relay import RENDEZVOUS_RELAY
from wormhole.xfer_util import send
APPID = u"lothar.com/wormhole/text-or-file-xfer"

def main(reactor, message):
    def got_code(code):
        print("code: {}".format(code))
    return send(reactor, APPID, RENDEZVOUS_RELAY, message, None,
                on_code=got_code)

react(main, sys.argv[1:])
```

The receiving side should be similar. Lemme know if this looks promising, I can help get those scripts get written.

> If you want to use pkg-config from Python, it might be worth considering pkgconfig

Unfortunately adding any dependency to setup.py basically makes the package uninstallable. pip currently does not care and runs setup.py without any dependencies installed. This makes it impossible (for example) to use Cython for building extensions without including the generated C source files.

The relevant issue in pip is this one: https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/1820
This has been resolved by adding a new optional `pyproject.toml` configuration file. A package that requires e.g. `pkgconfig` for building can not declare this in that file.

However, this seems to be a quite new feature of pip and I'd rather not make use of it until it is standard on most Python installations. Our build system routinely upgrades pip before installing anything else, but not everybody is doing that.

And running pkg-config using subprocess.Popen is not really a big deal IMHO.

Any news on this? 

Best regards,  Björn

```bash
$ cd ffmpeg-python/examples

$ source venv/bin/activate
(venv) $ pip install -r requirements.txt

```

Note that you need to do the `source venv/bin/activate` whenever you start a new shell, or use something like `virtualenvwrapper`.

So I've moved on from trying to get Django/Python to render my React apps. Instead, I use Django with Django REST Framework to serve my API, and then I have a Node.js server running that simply handles every request outside of <url>/api/.

Combined with Redux-router, React, etc., it means that when a user visits a URL, they get a complete server-rendered page with data preloaded from the Django-based API. Then once the React components are loaded on the client-side, they navigate "locally" with data being pulled from the API, never touching the Node.js server again. 

I went this way because it simplified how I developed the front-end application, since it's now completely React-based. It made little sense to have Django serve up a template just to pass off server-rendering to Node.js, and then come back.

So Redux & Redux-Router are fantastic, once you get them working correctly. With my new setup, Django does what it's best at, and Node does what it's best at, and the two services live separately.

In production, I run these in Docker containers, so it's even more modular.

Just my two cents. :-)

OK I found the reason. In `MANIFEST.in`, the egg-info directory is ignored, and these files are necessary for easy_install to work. Buildout uses easy_install, thus triggers the error.
This can be easily reproduced with the command:
```
$ easy_install -md /tmp http://pypi.python.org/packages/source/p/python-social-auth/python-social-auth-0.2.10.tar.gz
```
The fix is as easy as:
```diff
diff --git a/MANIFEST.in b/MANIFEST.in
index 2cf5b48..d2fef43 100644
--- a/MANIFEST.in
+++ b/MANIFEST.in
@@ -7,7 +7,6 @@ recursive-include social/tests *.txt
 graft examples
 
 recursive-exclude .tox *
-recursive-exclude python_social_auth.egg-info *
 recursive-exclude social *.pyc
 recursive-exclude examples *.pyc
 recursive-exclude examples *.db
```

exchangelib tries to automatically detect the server version, and will use the returned value in further requests. If you know what you're doing, you can set the version explicitly as explained in https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib#setup-and-connecting

You may simply be sending requests more frequently than Office365 is happy with, and this is their way of rate-limiting your account. `ServiceAccount` will gloss over much of this, but not magically make Office365 happier.

You could try one of these:
* Reduce your query complexity by requesting fewer fields, less items, shorter date ranges etc.
* Reduce your sync frequency
* Ask your Exchange admins to relax the throttling policies on your account
* Shrink the request pool:
```python
from exchangelib.protocol import BaseProtocol
BaseProtocol.SESSION_POOLSIZE = 1  # Default is 4
```

So, if you're using related models then, you first need to make sure that they're registered to recognise and follow the foreign key when revisions are saved. The easiest way to do this is to register them with VersionAdmin, with one being set up as an InlineAdmin of the other.

Assuming this is done, then you can revert to a previous revision using either the admin or the api. If you use the admin to roll back to a revision where the inline was not present, then you should see the 'delete' checkbox checked by the inline instance in the revert preview screen. If you then press the save button to confirm the revert, everything will be rolled back, and the inline instance will be gone.

If you're using the api to revert, as per you function, then you need to call version.revision.revert(delete=True). This will roll back to the revision and delete any extra objects that weren't in the revision. This parameter used to be in the docs, but looking at them now, it seems to have got lost at some point. I'll update them shortly.

If you're using reversion to load a previous version of an object, then it's worth noting that accessing the old object via version.object_version.object will give unpredictable results when accessing foreign keys and m2m relationships. Reversion is designed as a rollback service to recover old data, and doesn't allow you to access old data without first calling revert() on it.

Does this work for you now?

I have customized the Admin change_view to provide additional functionality, for example a `publish` method on an `Article` model. What my view does is to start a transaction, make the form save all its fields into the DB (form.save(commit=True)) and, then calls the `publish` method with the updated state. Thus, the `publish` method receives the potential final state before publication, it validates it (at the model level) and can further modify it. If validation fails, the transaction is aborted, if it succeeds it is confirmed. In summary, this view provides a mixed *save & publish* functionality.

In this setting if the `publish` method needs to know the initial value of some field, I should provide as a parameter the form initial data dictionary. So, after some digging seems SQL Server doesn't support nullable Foreign Key by default... Well, it does in a way but you can insert NULL only once.. Second insert will fail because NULL value is already present in the column and treated like a duplicate (which will throw an error since the column is tagged as unique). My current workaround is this:

```
CREATE TABLE dbo.parent(id INT PRIMARY KEY)
CREATE TABLE dbo.child(id INT PRIMARY KEY, parent_id INT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent(id))

CREATE UNIQUE NONCLUSTERED INDEX yes_it_can_be_nullable
ON dbo.child(parent_id)
WHERE parent_id IS NOT NULL;

insert into dbo.parent values (1),(2),(3),(4),(5)
insert into dbo.child values (1, NULL),(2, NULL),(3, 1),(4, 2),(5, 3)

insert into dbo.child values (6, 1)
-- THIS FAILS AS INTENDED
```
Hopefully this will help someone who's not experienced with SQL Server (same as me) until we get support for nullable Foreign Key.

It's not easy. Especially when you're distributed across Python processes, but the only way it can be solved is with a central cache. 

One possible solution is to be aware of how many Python nodes you have and change a setting accordingly. If you *know* you only have 1 node (e.g. 1 EC2 instance) you can use Python's builtin lock and if you know you have multiple, you opt for the use of a global cache. But still, even with that there is a race-condition risk if you two independent, near simultaneous, cache-as-a-lock requests come in at the same time.

> I needed threads because my web client makes a bunch of little AJAX calls in parallel and I am using Flask and didn't want to fiddle with figuring out the event soup. 

The green threads from eventlet are perfect for this use case. Did you know? Just monkey patch the Python standard library, then use your threads as you normally do, and eventlet switches them up for greenlets.

Your example is limited. For example, there is no easy way to implement callbacks. Implementing sending messages to specific users, or to a room would not be as hard, but you don't have support for that in the code above.

If you want to go the two server route, I recommend you use two physically separate servers, one for Flask, one for Flask-SocketIO, then use the message queue support already in the extension to be able to emit from the Flask side. This has full support for all the SocketIO features.

@siddhantgoel 

I agree that the limit maybe useful in other targets, I initially avoided doing this as I figured the framework being used or reverse proxy deployed in front of the app would have a MAX_REQUEST_SIZE check already and didn't want to duplicate that check. 

I figured the MAX_REQUEST_SIZE in the app/reverse proxy would be enough to protect against insanely large file uploads but the library would still be vulnerable to someone crafting form that put excessive amounts of data into form fields that was using a value target.

All that being said, moving the check to the parser would make it possible to report the field that caused the error as suggested by @kolomenkin so it is probably worth moving it on that basis alone. 

As for your suggestion about error handling I don't agree with the idea of continuing to parse the form if an error condition is met. I would advocate a more defensive approach. If the size of a field must be smaller than a specified size, as defined by the application's API, I would rather invalidate the request as soon as possible and not continue to consume server resources for the bad request. My intent was that the handler using this library would catch the exception and decide what to do with it. The limit is in place to protect the server, a request that exceeds the limit is an indication that the request maybe malicious. This is especially true if the server app developer also controls/develops the client code.

The problem is that the chromedriver does not search the `PATH` environment but only the default chrome installation paths. But you can explicitly pass a chrome/chromium binary to be used:
```Python
from selenium import webdriver
import chromedriver_binary
driver = webdriver.Chrome("/path/to/chrome")
```
Note that you have to pass the complete filename of your chrome/chromium binary and not just its containig folder (like you would do in the `PATH` variable).

@eric-haibin-lin True. We don't need to have graph executor or operators aware of kvstore. What we need to do is to register a function to NNVM for operators. What the function does is to return row ids for given input. The process is kind of similar to shape inference but is called when the inputs are ready. The graph executor calls these functions (if they are available) and accumulates all row ids required for the computation. Some component that is responsible for communicating with kvstore should send these row Ids to kvstore and fetch weights.

What I mean is to use an array to map row ids to the physical location of a row in the row sparse matrix, instead of using a hashtable. My point is that if you want to use `update_on_kvstore=False`, which costs memory of O(n * p), why not use an array to map row ids, which costs memory of O(n)?  It isn't expensive to maintain the map either, in terms of computation. The cost is O(nzr) if you do it correctly. I think maintaining this array map is strictly better than maintaining the entire weight matrix in every worker node, from the perspective of computation and memory overhead.

@KellenSunderland @marcoabreu @reminisce Hi, I think we should set the absolute error to one [ULP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_in_the_last_place). When we check whether the int8 data are equal, we should set the absolute error to 1.
In this case, we convert int32 data to float32 data and then convert to int8 data. In the last step `(np.sign(data) * np.minimum(np.abs(data) * scale + 0.5, quantized_range)).astype('int8')` plus 0.5 to realize rounded up will bring one ULP truncated error.
For example, MKLDNN float 63.4999 will not be round up to 64 after plus 0.5 but Numpy float 63.5000 will. The absolute error of float32 1e-4 will be expand to 1 after converted to int8.

I'm running into my original issue where I have a DAG like:

`LatestOnlyOperator -> op1 -> op2 -> ...`

If the latest only op skips, the op1 skips (as expected), but then the DAG is marked as failed before anything further downstream is looked at. Expected behavior is everything downstream is skipped and the DAG is marked as successful.

I do think the revert still makes sense, but just noting we may still need an additional fix, which could just be adding a `ALL_SUCCESS_OR_SKIPPED` trigger rule.

As mentioned on the JIRA, @gwax your analyses is not entirely correct. 

It is actually a bug. The transition is handled by Airflow, all dates should be stored in UTC regardless of what time zone database server thinks it is in. Note that for many databases the current time zone is dependent on the connection (MySQL) or connection and server setting (Postgres) or no support at all (SQLite). In addition none of these databases are really time zone aware as they don’t store the time zone itself (only Oracle does afaik). This means that transitions are not handled correctly if left to the database.

This means that func.now created an arbitrary date that we cannot rely on across workers/scheduler and it circumvented the check we do to only store UTC date times as it was executed at the database level. 

DAGS store the information on time zones and with pendulum (and not pytz) we manage transitions across time zones and dst.

You could argue that converting the D.B. fields to time zone aware fields was redundant, however I think it is an improvement across the board.

So the mantra is:

1. Everything in the database is required to be UTC
2. Airflow uses UTC internally
3. DAGs maintain time zone information
4. Only at next/previous schedule for Cron schedules we convert back the DAG time zone.
5. User interface (web) can therefore function time zone independent (not implemented)

This keeps things sane and is according to best practices.

In brave/finagle and some others, it is more about a separate trace context
object than a span one. For example, you can pass an unsampled context
(which may already be going on or else you'd not necessarily be able to
propagated unsampled). Ex in brave the noop span is just a wrapped context

>

Thanks for reporting Ads API SDK issues.

Ads API SDK is automatically generated, and we do have a process of marking deprecation and throw something like "this xxxx is going to deprecated, please use yyyy instead" error messages.

The problem here is that, deprecating of an enum value is more complicated than deprecating end points, fields, or parameters, and we currently don't have a programmatic way to detect it yet. While I agree that it would be great to check on the SDK level, we believe that since Ads API will eventually catch and throw the meaning error messages, this feature is not that critical.

I'll still mark this as an enhancement; please let us know if this feature is more critical for you than what we thought. Thanks a lot!

I spent a bunch of time looking into this. A web browser (Firefox, links, etc) will show glyphs for characters covered by this issue. There's no way anyone would look at that and want to copy/paste it.

However, printing `bleach.clean()` output to a terminal like this will interpret these characters causing the above problem because that's not a web browser rendering HTML--it's just printing a string to a terminal.

Regardless, it seems helpful to sanitize characters in Characters tokens by converting them to the relevant character entities.

Using the above example, after this change we'd get something like this:

```
>>> print(bleach.clean("a\ac"))
ac
```

I think that should work fine in web browsers, too. I'll verify that when I get there.

I'll try to get this done in the next few days and then do a Bleach 2.1 release.

This is likely to be very related: https://github.com/mozilla/airmozilla/issues/827#issuecomment-374274903
What happened there was that I was logged in (thank you django session cookies) but since my `oidc_id_token_expiration` has expired so mozilla_django_oidc redirects me to `/authorize?...&prompt=none` which then errors out and redirects back to `/oidc/callback` which redirects to `/login-failure/` (which is a public django page) and there the middleware kicks in and redirects back again. 

Above @willkg wrote this suggestion:

*"add handling in `/oidc/callback` for when the querystring params have `error` and `error_description` and redirect to the failure url carrying that error code along"*

I wonder if we should do something like this, in the `/oidc/callback`, instead: 
```python
if request.GET.get('error'):
    # Make sure the user is not logged in any more because the callback
    # was unhappy with the user. 
    if request.user.is_authenticated:  # XXX Do this the backwards compat way!
        auth.logout(request) 
```

Otherwise, if you allow the user to remain logged in on an old session cookie and Auth0 is grumpy the RefreshIDToken middleware will get stuck in a redirect loop.

So there is now a *real* implementation of this in the wild. [Tecken does it](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tecken/blob/master/tecken/useradmin/middleware.py). It's a middleware that talks to the Auth0 Management API and finds the user and if the user is found **and** is `blocked==true` then it makes the user account inactive, calls `auth.logout(request)`, and immediately returns `403 Forbidden`. It's throttled with the regular `django.core.cache.cache`. It's been looked at by @gdestuynder but no formal security sign-off. 

So, let's put a version of this into `mozilla-django-oidc`. Tecken is probably not the only site that wants to do server-to-server checking to see if someone is still a valid user. 

Points to think about or discuss:

1. This implementation requires that the Auth0 account has been extended with the additional config to allow the client ID to use the Auth0 Management API. (This was done offline by chatting on IRC with kang). This needs to be made much more clear in terms of documentation and possibly push "upstream" so that people can ask for this via The Hub. 

2. Having this extra config on your Auth0 account makes the client ID + secret a lot more powerful so only RPs who are really careful and vetted can/should do this. What needs to be done formally to back this up? We could simply *state* in the documentation "This only works if you have [Auth0 Management API config thing] enabled. Talk to your Auth0 account administrator."

3. Is this much better than doing refresh token redirect loops? If you can use this new trick, what's the point of sending the user on a redirect loop ever X hours? 

4. What should it be called? "auth0specific"? "contrib"? "proprietary?"

5. Is @gdestuynder 's team interested in building a wrapper web service around this to "tighten it down"?

6. If the user signs in once and gets a 1 year session cookie, we lose the ability to pick up refreshed user profile details (avatar, full name etc.) We *could* use the Auth0 Management API to do that. At least make it configurable or something. Is this interesting or a waste of attention?

7. Perhaps it should just log the user out (and return a `403 Forbidden`) and NOT change the user to `is_active=False`. What do you think?

8. Also, I made a [Django management command](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tecken/blob/master/tecken/useradmin/management/commands/is-blocked-in-auth0.py) which was useful during development. Is there interest in including this?

All, in all. Take a look at the implementation and say what you think about having it inside `mozilla-django-oidc`.

@rmorshea in my ideal world where the API was being designed from scratch, doing something like:
```
class MyClass(HasTraits):
    x = Integer() # Note that no default_value was set.

MyClass().x
```
would raise an error indicating that no default value was specified for `x`.

My argument for this behavior is that there is no value appropriate for use as a default value for a type that represents an integer field of an arbitrary object.  I agree with @minrk's sentiment that 0 is the only sane choice ***if we have to choose a default***.  I just don't think it's universal enough to be forced on every usage of `Integer` by default.  I'd rather have an API that asks me to clarify my intent than one that tries to guess what I mean without enough information to do so reliably.

In my experience, liberal default values tend to create bugs that manifest far away from their root causes, which at best wastes developer time tracking down where the bad value actually entered the system, and at worst encourages band-aid solutions that try to handle malformed values where they cause issues rather than where they're introduced.  

In the case of traitlets specifically, the failure-at-a-distance problem is made worse by the fact that `HasTraits` accepts arbitrary keyword arguments.  On more than one occasion, I've had the frustrating experience of tracing an unexpected 0 or False value in a trait to having made a typo in a traited object's constructor or config file.

It might be helpful to know what commit 332521a was attempting to fix.  It seems like we have traded something unknown to us being broken for the breakage described in this issue.  A quick check of some random diff options suggests that this behavior change will affect not only "-U" in git diff, but also "-C", "-M', and "-B".  These are likely to cause GitPython users to run into difficult-to-diagnose problems when they attempt to use these options in their customary form.

With an understanding of what 332521a was fixing, we could evaluate a way forward -- perhaps there is another way to accomplish what was intended there, or perhaps we need to support different behaviors for different git subcommands, or perhaps we need to say that single-char options with arguments are not supported and return a useful error to gitpython users.

The main issue here is that the `check_nix` function (see [scripts/checknix.py](https://github.com/G-Node/nixpy/blob/ff8a923921441e2377ebe72f89101a3e1ee67c24/scripts/checknix.py#L11)) isn't that great (it's actually quite stupid).

All it does, is is creates a a cpp file which includes `<nix.hpp>` and tries to compile it. If it succeeds, then `setup.py` just goes ahead and starts compiling the bindings.

If boost-python isn't installed on the system, this will cause an error.

The best fix for this issue is a general way to check if all the (C++) dependencies are present, so both nix and boost-python.  Issue #209 is also relevant here, in that we generally want dependencies and dependency versions to be checked before attempting to build the bindings.

For starters though, we could also accept a general, cross-platform solution that simply tells us if boost-python is available on the system.

* `imp` is deprecated since version 3.4, in favour of `importlib`.
  https://docs.python.org/3/library/imp.html

* `importlib` is available in Python 2.7 as "a minor subset of what is available in the more full-featured package of the same name from Python 3.1 that provides a complete implementation of `import`. What is here has been provided to help ease in transitioning from 2.7 to 3.1."
  https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/importlib.html

There are other parameters to `setup()` that don't handle unicode values well, and unfortunately, the [code is in distutils](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/distutils/dist.py#L606-L608) and not setuptools, so can't be readily fixed here.

I've taken to not using `from __future__ import unicode_literals` in my setup.py scripts, but you have other options:

1. Wrap your value in `str`, i.e. `keywords=str("multiprocessing,parallel,workers,...")`. Note that keyword values [should be separated by commas](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/apiref.html#distutils.core.setup), despite setuptools' bad example.
2. Supply the values already split, i.e. `keywords=['multiprocessing', 'parallel', 'workers', ...]`. I'm not certain this approach won't have other consequences down the line.

I definitely don't recommend using `b''` as the workaround, as that will almost certainly cause you problems on Python 3, and though you may not be targetting Python 3 now, it's bad practice to be using bytes where text is intended.

Thanks for putting this together @danfrankj . This is indeed something along the lines of one of the approaches what we chatted about in #59; so thanks for giving it a shot. I gave it a bit more thought, and think we should probably just add a new database duct kind called 'nosql', and commit to some fairly common set of methods (get, set, find,... maybe others? We can always add more in the future). The only reason I have not already done this, although I have thought in this direction (thus #19),  is that I don't actually have a whole lot of hands on experience with NoSQL databases, and am thus not sure which set of methods should actually be implemented. Is `get`, `set` and `find` enough? What other things do you think would be useful?

Doing it this way allows us to commit to a standard API (like our other duct types), including as part of that API a particular attribute which is the raw client here being wrapped (and as such, we wouldn't actually need to wrap it). This leads to a more consistent user experience, particularly when accessed via the DuctRegistry and its proxies.

There may still be some utility to having a `WrappedClient` Duct subclass, even if it is not used here, and we can add it to the utils submodule.

Happy to be convinced otherwise if you think this is not the right approach. What are your thoughts?

```python
from pykafka.common import OffsetType
consumer = topic.get_simple_consumer(consumer_group="testgroup",
                                                                   auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.EARLIEST,
                                                                   reset_offset_on_start=True)
```

Hi there,

This isn't actually an issue, but a consequence of how Storm is designed.  `next_tuple` is being continuously called because the Storm worker is constantly asking for more tuples.

There are a few ways you can solve the issue.  First, you can increase bolt parallelism so that your bolts are able to handle the volume produced by the spout. In practice, this is very difficult because of how insanely fast Kafka is.  If you have a substantial backlog in Kafka, it's almost always going to swamp your workers.

Ultimately, the setting you want is `topology.max.spout.pending`.  This will control the number of pending tuples that each spout has within the topology.  The default in Storm is to have this unlimited, which is why it keeps asking for more tuples.  If you have it turned on, then it'll stop asking for tuples when the topology is "full".  To use this setting, just add this to your sparse call: `-o topology.max.spout.pending=SOME_NUMBER`

> For pyregion, I guess the question is what should be its behavior when some of the input points are not defined in the image coordinate. My inclination is that we just raise an exception indicating that the filter is ill-defined. Thought?

I think for operations that act on arrays the common idiom is to return `NaN` for items where no result is available. That's e.g. what you get from `WCS` for pixels where the transformation isn't defined. So I'm -1 on raising an exception, because that means users don't get any output. Numpy emits warnings:
```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.sqrt([3, -2])
RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in sqrt
array([ 1.73205081,         nan])
```
which in my experience are more often annoying and have to be suppressed instead of helpful, but emitting warnings is certainly also a valid way to deal with this.

I unfortunately need to disagree with you on the ransac point. While 3 points is the minimum for a match, It is not unlikely for 3 random points to line up nicely, and incorrectly, as a simple translation when the real change is both a translation and a rotation. 

In fact, encountering this issue is the very reason I made this change, so it isn't theoretical.

Requiring more points to match up consistently means that the probability of an incorrect but consistent estimate is correspondingly lower, particularly when the data provides enough correct results that you won't have trouble finding 8 points for which the transform is consistent.

So, let's put some more context in there.

In case we want to build a generic *cliquet* client, it is important to have the *cliquet* protocol defined somewhere, in order to know if the client we're using is actually compatible with the version of the protocol.

For instance, let's say we change the way we handle pagination, we would need the client to know beforehand that the old version isn't supported.

Currently, cliquet only exposes the version of the application (what is in the setup.py) and as such doesn't convey this protocol information.

So, ideally, I think we should display the two information: version of the app *and* version of the protocol, since they're two different things.

@mfyang thanks a lot for reporting! I've just fixed it.

The main reason why we use POST method for activation is that we think GET method in any API should **not** change models state in database.

The other reason is this is just a REST API and opening the URL pointing to `ActivationView` in the browser doesn't make sense, because it replies with JSON (or other format). Assuming that you work for example on Single Page App the recommended way to handle the activation process is to create a separate page where the app triggers POST request to your backend API.

That's why we stick to this approach. However we are open to suggestions.

The way normal fastboot flash works is to send 2 commands:

* `download:%(size)d` followed by `size` bytes
* `flash:%(partition)s`

From what I gather with the code, they do this instead:

* `getvar max-download-size` to know the 'sparse limit'
* `download:%(total_size)d` followed by the data in up to `max-download-size` byte chunks
* `flash:%(partition)s`

However, the major factor here is what the data is in the second case. There are a bunch of calls into `libsparse` to generate the sparse image. If we're lucky, we can get that data and then send it over correctly. The code that seem relevant here is [sparse's img2simg.c](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/system/core/+/master/libsparse/img2simg.c) which may do it for us, though here is the [initial sparse-file entry point in fastboot](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/system/core.git/+/master/fastboot/fastboot.cpp#641)

What your code is doing is overwriting the data that will be flashed with the latest chunk, so you will end up with the end of your bootloader flashed to the front of the partition.

Looks great, thanks @ahgnaw. One more nitpicky comment, commits should be prefixed like this: 
```
API: an (incompatible) API change
BLD: change related to building numpy
BUG: bug fix
DEP: deprecate something, or remove a deprecated object
DEV: development tool or utility
DOC: documentation
ENH: enhancement
MAINT: maintenance commit (refactoring, typos, etc.)
REV: revert an earlier commit
STY: style fix (whitespace, PEP8)
TST: addition or modification of tests
REL: related to releasing numpy
```
